exemplar.title: To what extent has the city of Halifax followed up with plans to preserve the Old South Suburb Heritage Conservation District, including the renewal and preservation of Municipally Registered Heritage Buildings?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the city of Halifax followed up with plans to preserve the Old South Suburb Heritage Conservation District, including the renewal and preservation of Municipally Registered Heritage Buildings?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, well-formulated geographical question linked to urban decay/renewal theory
Precise study area identification with feasibility for primary data collection
Multiple locator and analytical maps following key mapping conventions
weaknesses: Some individual maps lack legends, scale bars or north arrows
Rationale for selecting only four of thirty-four policies is not fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the top-band descriptors: the question is explicitly geographical with clear theoretical links, the location is precisely defined and feasible, and mapping conventions are largely met. Minor lapses in map conventions and sampling rationale do not outweigh the criterionâs overall excellence, warranting full marks (3).
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods clearly described and directly linked to each hypothesis
weaknesses: Omission of details on randomisation, pilot testing and inter-observer reliability
Scoring protocol for the environmental quality survey is not fully explained
Reliance on subjective measures without reliability checks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are described with clear relevance to the research question and data collection techniques are correctly applied, but explanations lack depth (randomisation, scoring clarity) and data quality controls are minimal. This aligns with band 2, meriting 2 marks.

strengths: All collected data are directly relevant to the hypotheses and policies
Appropriate use of tables, graphs and thematic maps
Most mapping and figure conventions (legends, titles) are observed
weaknesses: Inconsistent figure numbering and duplicated captions
Graphs lack axis titles and units
Table and bibliography formatting errors (colspan mis-match, citation inconsistencies)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance is high and presentation methods are appropriate. Occasional errors in numbering, labeling and formatting prevent top marks. The work sits firmly in the mid-band (3), reflecting competent treatment with some convention lapses.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive analysis effectively linked to each hypothesis and to urban renewal theory
Anomalies and qualitative observations (e.g., tree presence) are identified
Photographs are well integrated to support interpretations
weaknesses: No use of statistical tests or confidence measures
Limited theoretical depth in trend explanations
Analytical gaps in quantifying policy implementation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis uses appropriate descriptive techniques and links patterns to the research question, addressing all hypotheses. However, the absence of statistical rigor and deeper theoretical discussion limits the work to the mid-band of 3â4. A score of 4 reflects solid performance above minimal requirements without comprehensive statistical analysis.

strengths: Conclusion clearly addresses and supports each hypothesis with evidence from the analysis
Coherent, concise statement fully grounded in results
weaknesses: Lacks a quantitative summary of overall policy implementation extent
Could more directly restate the answer to the overarching research question
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, coherent and fully supported by the preceding analysis, meeting the top descriptor for this criterion (band 2). Minor missing summary detail does not detract from its clarity, so it earns 2 marks.

strengths: Balanced identification of methodological strengths and weaknesses
Relevant suggestions for improvement with brief anticipated benefits
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks depth in exploring broader factors (e.g., temporal variability)
Improvement suggestions are not fully elaborated in terms of impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation provides an appropriate outline of strengths, weaknesses and relevant improvements, but does not achieve comprehensive explanation and depth. This aligns with band 2, justifying 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple maps are provided: national and city-scale locator maps, a hand-drawn district boundary map, and GIS-style analytical maps with clear legends, symbols and north arrows.  Although hand-labelled lettering is informal, mapping conventions (scale bar, legend, orientation) are present and readable, so the strand fits the top descriptor.

The fieldwork question is explicitly geographical, focuses on urban conservation within a clearly defined district, and is directly related to Option G: Urban Environments.  It is worded to allow measurable follow-up (extent to which plans were implemented) and the student establishes three testable hypotheses.  The clear theoretical link to urban decay/renewal enables hypothesis formation, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

Data are presented through tables, bar graph, radar diagram and thematic maps, which are appropriate and mostly effective.  However, some figures are duplicated or mis-numbered and graph axes lack units, so presentation is competent but not the âmost effectiveâ, placing it in band 3-4 (score 3).

Data collection produced first-hand scores for 32 buildings and observations along four streets; techniques (photography, EQS) were correctly applied.  Nevertheless, the survey is acknowledged as subjective, no inter-observer reliability check was used, and side streets were omitted, so the data quality is acceptable but not âhigh-qualityâ, meriting band 2.

Analysis relies on descriptive commentary, simple counts and qualitative comparison; no statistical tests (e.g., Chi-square, Spearman) or confidence levels are used.  Techniques are therefore appropriate but basic, fitting the 3-4 band (score 3).

The student links patterns (presence/absence of trees, distribution of EQ scores) to each hypothesis and to urban renewal theory.  Anomalies are noted, but deeper theoretical discussion is limited; thus interpretation is sound yet not comprehensive, matching the 3-4 band.

The student locates the study area precisely (Old South Suburb HCD, Halifax) and justifies feasibility by referencing 32 municipally registered buildings and four named streets.  Primary data collection on site is demonstrably possible and was undertaken; therefore the work meets the highest band for location specificity.

Evaluation identifies key strengths (systematic transects, quantitative EQS) and weaknesses (subjectivity, limited street coverage).  Discussion is relevant but not in-depth; factors such as temporal variability or broader methodological alternatives are unexplored, aligning with band 2.

All collected data directly address the policies and hypotheses, and sample size (32 properties, four transects) is adequate for partial analysis, though not exhaustive.  This fits the middle band (most data relevant, allowing partialânot fullâanalysis).

The conclusion restates whether each hypothesis is accepted/rejected and refers to specific policy evidence, showing clear support from preceding analysis.  It is concise and coherent, fulfilling the highest available band for this criterion (band 2).

All three hypotheses are addressed, but sample limitations and absence of statistical significance leave noticeable analytical gaps (e.g., no quantitative measure of âextentâ).  Hence the analysis is adequate with some omissions, warranting band 3-4 (score 3).

Methods (environmental quality survey, systematic transects, policy sampling) are described with explicit links to each hypothesis and policy.  However, explanations lack full detail on scoring protocols, randomisation or pilot testing, so they are clear but not fully comprehensive, aligning with band 2.

Legends, north arrows and titles are present on all maps, but figure numbering is inconsistent and two figures share the same caption.  These occasional errors mean conventions are not flawless yet more than adequate, matching the 3-4 band (score 3).

Improvement suggestions are specific (use multiple observers, include all streets, investigate all 34 policies) and briefly state anticipated benefits (reduced bias, greater coverage).  Impact explanations are present but not richly developed, so the work fits the middle band.

annotations.comment: Hypotheses list is clear but rationale for selecting only four of thirty-four policies is not justified; consider describing sampling representativeness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Methodology description mentions systematic approach but omits details on randomisation and pilot testing. Expand to enhance reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Table 1âs colspan attribute does not match the number of columns displayed; correct structure to adhere to table conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Bar graph lacks axis titles and units; label axes clearly to improve interpretability of data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The studentâs fieldwork question is clearly geographical, precisely formulated and linked to urban decay/renewal theory, enabling hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The title is succinct and identifies the IB Geography focus, aiding reader orientation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Reliance on generative AI for idea brainstorming should be balanced with traditional literature review to ensure methodological rigor.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Locator maps follow conventions with scale and orientation. This aids spatial context and feasibility of primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The environmental quality survey table presents clear criteria but does not explain how scores aggregate; include scoring protocol to ensure clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Ethical practice noted: omitting people from photographs maintains anonymity. This strengthens data integrity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Linking Figure 4 map results to Hypothesis 2 is effective, illustrating direct geospatial analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Interpretation assumes no tree presence exceptions; consider verifying small-scale plantings or maintenance periods before concluding.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Overhead wires discussion is descriptive but lacks quantification; include counts or percentages for stronger support.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: General policy follow-through statement could be strengthened by quantifying how many policies were implemented.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Environmental quality bar graph would benefit from error bars or confidence intervals to reflect data reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure numbering is duplicated (âFigure 5â appears twice), causing confusion; harmonise captions and numbering.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Photograph annotation (Figure 5.1) includes detailed qualitative observations, linking visuals to survey scores effectively.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Classification of score ranges for this building lacks clarity; explicitly reference table ranges to avoid misalignment.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Photographs of 1236 Barrington St are well integrated, illustrating mid-range survey outcomes clearly.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: When discussing building scores, reference overall sample size and distribution to contextualise individual case studies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Photograph caption numbering is inconsistent; ensure figures and captions align to avoid reader confusion.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Second bibliography entry lacks publisher details and consistent date format; ensure full referencing to uphold academic conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Consider applying basic statistical tests (e.g., Chi-square) to assess significance of observed trends in environmental quality.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Table 2 header cells are excessively verbose; abbreviate or use footnotes to maintain readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Map examples lack a legend and scale bar, making it hard to interpret symbol meanings and distances.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 8 shows good spatial detail but lacks north arrow and legend; include both for full mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Conclusion clearly addresses each hypothesis and is supported by analysis, demonstrating coherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Enhance the conclusion by summarising the overall extent (e.g., percentage of policies implemented) to directly answer the research question.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Evaluation acknowledges subjectivity and sampling strengths, providing balanced critique.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Future evaluations could consider temporal variation (e.g., seasonal maintenance cycles) to deepen methodological reflection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Extension to examine all thirty-four policies is insightful and would significantly improve comprehensiveness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Bibliography entries show inconsistencies in style (e.g., capitalization, author formatting); adopt a single citation convention.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
